Title: Induction of collective behavior by β-1,3-glucans in microalgae

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Degrades β-1,3-glucans (laminarin-like glucans), thereby reducing extracellular/cell-wall β-1,3-glucan accumulation associated with aggregation.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: competes with target-product accumulation by removing β-1,3-glucans required for aggregation
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Promotes stress-induced cell aggregation/collective behavior in Chlamydomonas.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Cell wall; often at intersections between aggregated cells
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of BGL1 causes spontaneous aggregation and β-1,3-glucan accumulation, indicating BGL1 negatively regulates aggregation by degrading β-1,3-glucans.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: CLiP insertion mutant genotyping; RT-PCR loss-of-expression verification; spontaneous aggregation phenotyping; aniline blue β-1,3-glucan detection/quantification
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BGL1 is down-regulated in aggregating mutants and its knockout causes spontaneous aggregation. 2. bgl1 accumulates detectable β-1,3-glucans absent from isolated wild-type cells. 3. BGL1 therefore acts as a negative regulator of aggregation through β-1,3-glucan degradation.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Down-regulated in secretome of aggregating saz mutants
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Down-regulated during aggregation context; absent in bgl1 mutant
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: BGL1 and BGL2 are tandem genes and possible functional redundancy cannot be ruled out; many BGLs inferred to carry N-terminal signal peptides in comparative analysis.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Quantitative proteomics/secretome (previous study cited); RNA-seq in current study for laminarin response but not gene-specific for BGL1
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Reducing BGL1 activity could be used to induce controllable aggregation in microalgal biotechnology to facilitate harvesting, but may alter stress-response behavior.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Degrades β-1,3-glucans, limiting their accumulation and suppressing aggregation.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: competes with target-product accumulation by removing β-1,3-glucans required for aggregation
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Promotes stress-induced cell aggregation/collective behavior in Chlamydomonas.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Cell wall; often at intersections between aggregated cells
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of BGL2 causes spontaneous aggregation and β-1,3-glucan accumulation, indicating BGL2 negatively regulates aggregation by degrading β-1,3-glucans.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: CLiP insertion mutant genotyping; RT-PCR loss-of-expression verification; spontaneous aggregation phenotyping; aniline blue β-1,3-glucan detection/quantification
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BGL2 knockout leads to spontaneous aggregation under standard conditions. 2. β-1,3-glucans accumulate in bgl2 aggregates. 3. BGL2 functions as a negative regulator of β-1,3-glucan-dependent aggregation.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Down-regulated in secretome of aggregating saz mutants
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Down-regulated during aggregation context; absent in bgl2 mutant
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: BGL1 and BGL2 are tandem genes and possible redundancy cannot be excluded.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Quantitative proteomics/secretome (previous study cited); RNA-seq in current study for laminarin response but not gene-specific for BGL2
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Suppressing BGL2 may help trigger aggregate formation for easier biomass recovery in algal production systems.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Synthesizes β-1,3-glucans required for stress-induced aggregation in Chlamydomonas.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: UDP-glucose or glucose donor for β-1,3-glucan synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Acts as an elicitor and structural mediator of stress-induced aggregation/collective behavior.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Cell wall; base of flagella early after heat shock; intersections between cells in aggregates
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: BGS1 is essential for aggregation under heat shock and rose bengal stress; bgs1 loses aggregation and shows no β-1,3-glucan accumulation, with partial rescue by exogenous laminarin.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key control step supported; essential component for aggregate formation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Homology-based candidate identification; CLiP insertion mutant genotyping; RT-PCR loss-of-expression verification; stress-induced aggregation assays; laminarin rescue; aniline blue β-1,3-glucan detection/quantification
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BGS1 was identified as a putative β-1,3-glucan synthase required for aggregation. 2. bgs1 fails to aggregate under heat shock or rose bengal, unlike wild type. 3. Exogenous laminarin partially restores aggregation in bgs1, linking BGS1 function directly to β-1,3-glucan production.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Absent in bgs1 mutant; otherwise not reported
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Heat shock (50°C, 3 min); rose bengal (4 μM); exogenous laminarin
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Identified using homology to PtBGS from Phaeodactylum tricornutum; exogenous laminarin only partially rescues phenotype, suggesting endogenous glucan structure/availability matters.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss abolishes aggregation response; engineering may alter stress adaptation and collective behavior.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq of laminarin-treated cells; accession not provided in text
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Overexpressing or tuning BGS1 may be a route to induce nonlethal aggregation for microalgal harvesting and chassis engineering.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Contributes to β-1,3-glucan biosynthesis associated with aggregation, but is not strictly essential under tested conditions.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: UDP-glucose or glucose donor for β-1,3-glucan synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: bgs2 retains only partial aggregation under stress, indicating a contributory but nonessential role in β-1,3-glucan-dependent aggregation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: CLiP insertion mutant genotyping; RT-PCR loss-of-expression verification; heat shock and rose bengal aggregation assays
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BGS2 is a putative β-1,3-glucan synthase homolog. 2. bgs2 mutants still aggregate partially under inducing stress. 3. BGS2 contributes to, but is not essential for, aggregate formation.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Absent in bgs2 mutant; otherwise not reported
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Heat shock (50°C, 3 min); rose bengal (4 μM)
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: One of several BGS paralogs in C. reinhardtii; widespread homologs found across algal and plant groups in comparative analysis.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq of laminarin-treated cells; accession not provided in text
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Could be considered in gene stacking with BGS1 to fine-tune glucan-dependent aggregation.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Contributes to β-1,3-glucan biosynthesis associated with aggregation, but is not strictly essential under tested conditions.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: UDP-glucose or glucose donor for β-1,3-glucan synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: bgs3 retains partial aggregation under stress, indicating a supportive but nonessential role in β-1,3-glucan-dependent aggregation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: CLiP insertion mutant genotyping; RT-PCR loss-of-expression verification; heat shock and rose bengal aggregation assays
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BGS3 was identified as a putative β-1,3-glucan synthase candidate. 2. bgs3 mutants are still able to aggregate partially under inducing conditions. 3. BGS3 contributes to aggregate formation but is less decisive than BGS1.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Absent in bgs3 mutant; otherwise not reported
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Heat shock (50°C, 3 min); rose bengal (4 μM)
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Part of expanded BGS family in Chlamydomonas; comparative genomics suggests family diversification in Chlamydomonadalean evolution.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq of laminarin-treated cells; accession not provided in text
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential auxiliary engineering target for modulating degree of aggregation together with BGS1.
